note: Supplement or replace with your own verified schedule data. All ages are in weeks/months from date of birth unless otherwise noted.

vaccines.name: BCG (Tuberculosis)
vaccines.description: BCG vaccine protects against severe forms of tuberculosis. Given at birth.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Tuberculosis (TB) is still common in India. This vaccine helps protect your baby from serious TB infections affecting the brain and organs.
vaccines.what_to_expect: A small bump or scar may appear on the arm within weeks. This is normal.

vaccines.name: Hepatitis B (1st dose)
vaccines.description: First dose of Hepatitis B vaccine. Given at birth.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Hepatitis B can cause serious liver disease. Starting at birth gives the best protection.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Mild soreness at injection site is normal.

vaccines.name: OPV (Oral Polio Vaccine) — Birth dose
vaccines.description: First oral polio vaccine dose. Given at birth.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Polio can cause permanent paralysis. Even one drop of this vaccine helps protect your child.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Given orally (drops in mouth). No injection.

vaccines.name: Pentavalent (DPT+HepB+Hib) — 1st dose
vaccines.description: Combined vaccine protecting against 5 diseases. Given at 6 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Protects against Diphtheria, Pertussis (whooping cough), Tetanus, Hepatitis B, and Hib meningitis — all serious and potentially fatal diseases in young children.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Mild fever, fussiness, or soreness at injection site for 1-2 days is normal.

vaccines.name: Pentavalent (DPT+HepB+Hib) — 2nd dose
vaccines.description: Second dose of Pentavalent vaccine. Given at 10 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Each dose builds stronger protection. Missing doses means incomplete protection.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Similar mild reactions as first dose are normal.

vaccines.name: Pentavalent (DPT+HepB+Hib) — 3rd dose
vaccines.description: Third and final primary dose of Pentavalent vaccine. Given at 14 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Completes the primary immunization series. Full protection only after all 3 doses.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Mild fever or soreness is normal.

vaccines.name: OPV (Oral Polio) — 1st dose
vaccines.description: Second oral polio dose. Given at 6 weeks with Pentavalent.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Each dose increases protection against all three types of polio virus.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Oral drops. No side effects expected.

vaccines.name: OPV (Oral Polio) — 2nd dose
vaccines.description: Third oral polio dose. Given at 10 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Continues building immunity against polio.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Oral drops. No side effects expected.

vaccines.name: OPV (Oral Polio) — 3rd dose
vaccines.description: Fourth oral polio dose. Given at 14 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Completes primary polio vaccination series.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Oral drops. No side effects expected.

vaccines.name: Rotavirus Vaccine — 1st dose
vaccines.description: First Rotavirus dose. Given at 6 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Rotavirus causes severe diarrhea and dehydration in infants — a leading cause of child deaths in India.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Given orally. Mild stomach upset possible.

vaccines.name: Rotavirus Vaccine — 2nd dose
vaccines.description: Second Rotavirus dose. Given at 10 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Builds stronger protection against rotavirus diarrhea.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Oral drop. Mild stomach upset possible.

vaccines.name: Rotavirus Vaccine — 3rd dose
vaccines.description: Third Rotavirus dose. Given at 14 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Completes Rotavirus vaccination for full protection.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Oral drop.

vaccines.name: IPV (Inactivated Polio Vaccine) — 1st dose
vaccines.description: First injectable polio vaccine. Given at 6 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: IPV provides additional immunity especially in the spinal cord, strengthening OPV protection.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild soreness at site is normal.

vaccines.name: IPV (Inactivated Polio Vaccine) — 2nd dose
vaccines.description: Second IPV dose. Given at 14 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Completes injectable polio protection series.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild soreness is normal.

vaccines.name: PCV (Pneumococcal) — 1st dose
vaccines.description: First Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine dose. Given at 6 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Protects against pneumonia, meningitis, and ear infections caused by pneumococcal bacteria.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever or fussiness for 1-2 days.

vaccines.name: PCV (Pneumococcal) — 2nd dose
vaccines.description: Second PCV dose. Given at 14 weeks.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Builds stronger pneumococcal immunity.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever or fussiness for 1-2 days.

vaccines.name: PCV (Pneumococcal) — Booster
vaccines.description: PCV booster dose. Given at 9 months.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Booster significantly strengthens long-term pneumococcal protection.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever or fussiness for 1-2 days.

vaccines.name: Measles-Rubella (MR) — 1st dose
vaccines.description: First Measles-Rubella vaccine. Given at 9 to 12 months.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Measles can cause serious complications including blindness, brain damage, and death. Rubella is dangerous during pregnancy.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever and rash 7-12 days later are normal immune responses.

vaccines.name: Vitamin A — 1st dose
vaccines.description: First Vitamin A supplementation dose. Given at 9 months.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Vitamin A deficiency can cause blindness and increases susceptibility to infections. This supplement is critical for child growth.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Given orally. No side effects expected.

vaccines.name: Measles-Rubella (MR) — 2nd dose
vaccines.description: Second MR dose. Given at 16 to 24 months.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Provides complete and long-lasting measles and rubella protection.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever and rash 7-12 days later are normal.

vaccines.name: DPT Booster — 1st
vaccines.description: First DPT booster. Given at 16 to 24 months.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Renews protection against Diphtheria, Pertussis, and Tetanus as immunity from primary series wanes.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever or soreness.

vaccines.name: OPV Booster
vaccines.description: OPV booster dose. Given at 16 to 24 months.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Boosts polio immunity as part of India's polio eradication program.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Oral drops. No side effects expected.

vaccines.name: DPT Booster — 2nd (School entry)
vaccines.description: Second DPT booster at 5 to 6 years. Given at school entry.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Maintains protection as the child enters school and is exposed to more people.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild fever or arm soreness is normal.

vaccines.name: Tetanus-Diphtheria (Td) — 10 years
vaccines.description: Td vaccine at 10 years of age.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Renews protection against Tetanus and Diphtheria for older children.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild soreness at injection site.

vaccines.name: Tetanus-Diphtheria (Td) — 16 years
vaccines.description: Td vaccine at 16 years of age.
vaccines.why_it_matters: Final adolescent booster for Tetanus and Diphtheria before adulthood.
vaccines.what_to_expect: Injection. Mild soreness at injection site.
